topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — Non-GIPS Compliant Firms
stem:
Consider the following practices employed by a firm that does NOT comply with the GIPS standards when presenting performance history to prospective clients:

I. Presenting the performance of a composite of similar portfolios rather than using a single representative account
II. Excluding terminated accounts from composite performance history to avoid downward skew in reported returns
III. Using one top-performing account as a single representative proxy for the firm's overall performance record

How many of the above practices would satisfy the firm's obligations under Standard III(D)?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: Standard III(D) permits composites, requires terminated-account inclusion, and prohibits single-representative-account presentations

A is correct. The SPH states that firms not complying with the GIPS standards can meet Standard III(D) obligations by, among other things, "presenting the performance of a composite of similar portfolios rather than using a single representative account" and "including terminated accounts as part of any composite performance history." Item I qualifies — using a composite satisfies III(D). Item II does not qualify — excluding terminated accounts directly contradicts the explicit requirement to include them. Item III does not qualify — it is precisely the single-representative-account approach the SPH prohibits in favor of composites. Only Item I satisfies the standard.

Option B is incorrect because it implies two items satisfy the standard. Item II (excluding terminated accounts) is explicitly prohibited; the SPH requires their inclusion. Item III (single representative account) is also explicitly prohibited; the SPH requires a composite instead. Only one item satisfies the obligations.

Option C is incorrect because all three items do not satisfy Standard III(D). Items II and III each violate specific guidance in the SPH: terminated accounts must be included, and a single representative account may not substitute for a composite presentation.

topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — GIPS Compliance Claim Requirements
stem:
Consider the following statements about a firm that claims compliance with the GIPS standards:

I. The firm must asset-weight portfolio returns when calculating composite returns
II. The firm may use simple-average (equal) weighting of portfolio returns within a composite if the composite contains fewer than ten portfolios
III. The firm must comply with GIPS requirements addressing the treatment of material errors in performance presentations

How many of the above statements accurately describe the firm's obligations under the GIPS standards as discussed in the SPH?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: GIPS claims require asset-weighting and material-error compliance; no exception exists for small composites

B is correct. The SPH states in the Judd example: "The GIPS standards require firms to asset weight portfolio returns to calculate composite returns." No exception for small composites is mentioned. Item I is therefore accurate. The SPH also establishes from the Yao example that when a firm claims GIPS compliance, the member "must also comply with the GIPS standards requirements addressing the treatment of material errors." Item III is therefore accurate. Item II is inaccurate — the SPH specifies asset-weighting with no carve-out for composites of any size. Two items (I and III) satisfy the criterion.

Option A is incorrect because two statements accurately describe GIPS obligations, not just one. Both asset-weighting (Item I) and material-error compliance (Item III) are explicitly required by the SPH.

Option C is incorrect because Item II introduces a size-based exemption from asset-weighting that has no basis in the SPH. The requirement to asset-weight composite returns applies without qualification.

topic: Standard I(C) Misrepresentation — GIPS Material Error Treatment
stem:
Consider the following statements regarding a member whose firm claims GIPS compliance and has distributed performance materials containing an error:

I. A member who unknowingly distributes a performance presentation containing a typographical error before the mistake is discovered has not violated Standard I(C)
II. Once an error in a distributed GIPS-compliant performance report is discovered, the member must cease distribution of the incorrect material and correct the error by informing recipients
III. A member whose firm claims GIPS compliance is not subject to any GIPS requirements regarding error correction as long as the error is typographical rather than methodological

How many of the above statements are accurate according to the SPH?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: unintentional errors do not violate I(C), but discovery triggers GIPS material-error requirements; typographical errors are not exempt from GIPS error treatment rules

B is correct. The SPH's Yao example states: "Because Yao did not knowingly make the misrepresentation, however, he did not violate Standard I(C)." Item I is accurate. The same example also states: "Once the error is discovered, Yao must take steps to cease distribution of the incorrect material and correct the error by informing those who have received the erroneous information." Item II is therefore accurate. Item III is inaccurate — the SPH states that because the firm claims GIPS compliance, Yao "must also comply with the GIPS standards requirements addressing the treatment of material errors," with no exemption for typographical errors. Two items (I and II) are accurate.

Option A is incorrect because two statements are accurate. The requirement to cease distribution and correct errors (Item II) is explicitly stated alongside the absence of an I(C) violation for unintentional pre-discovery distribution (Item I).

Option C is incorrect because Item III inaccurately introduces a typographical-versus-methodological distinction that does not appear in the SPH. GIPS material-error requirements apply regardless of the error's origin.

topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — Explicit Non-GIPS Compliance Practices
stem:
Consider the following practices that the SPH explicitly lists as methods by which firms not complying with the GIPS standards may satisfy their Standard III(D) obligations:

I. Presenting the performance of a composite of similar portfolios rather than using a single representative account
II. Including terminated accounts as part of any composite performance history
III. Maintaining the data and records used to calculate the performance being presented

How many of the above practices are explicitly enumerated in the SPH as satisfying Standard III(D) for non-GIPS-compliant firms?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the SPH enumerates a specific list of five practices; all three items correspond directly to items on that list

C is correct. The SPH explicitly states that firms not complying with GIPS can meet Standard III(D) obligations by, among other things: "presenting the performance of a composite of similar portfolios rather than using a single representative account" (Item I), "including terminated accounts as part of any composite performance history" (Item II), and "maintaining the data and records used to calculate the performance being presented" (Item III). Each item maps directly to a practice listed in the SPH. All three satisfy the criterion.

Option A is incorrect because all three practices — not merely one — are explicitly enumerated in the SPH. Selecting only Item I would ignore the equally explicit requirements for terminated accounts and record maintenance.

Option B is incorrect because all three items qualify, not just two. The SPH lists maintaining records and including terminated accounts alongside the composite-presentation requirement as co-equal methods of satisfying Standard III(D).

topic: Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis — GIPS Compliance in Subadviser Selection
stem:
Consider the following statements concerning the role of GIPS compliance in the selection of external advisers or subadvisers under Standard V(A):

I. GIPS compliance is among the CFA Institute guides that the SPH identifies as criteria members and candidates may use when selecting external advisers
II. Under conditions of tight time constraints, relying solely on a subadviser's GIPS compliance and recent total return performance satisfies the due diligence required by Standard V(A)
III. The SPH indicates that GIPS compliance alone is insufficient — members must also thoroughly investigate firms and their operations before making a subadviser recommendation

How many of the above statements accurately reflect the SPH's guidance?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: GIPS is one factor supporting adviser evaluation, not a standalone sufficient basis regardless of time pressure

B is correct. The SPH lists "the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®)" among the CFA Institute guides available as criteria for selecting external advisers — confirming Item I. In the Jackson example, the SPH concludes: "By considering only performance and GIPS compliance, Jackson did not conduct sufficient review of potential firms to satisfy the requirements of Standard V(A). Jackson must thoroughly investigate the firms and their operations" — confirming Item III. Item II is inaccurate: the SPH's explicit treatment of the Jackson case establishes that time constraints do not excuse a member from thorough investigation; GIPS compliance combined with return data alone is insufficient. Items I and III are accurate.

Option A is incorrect because two statements reflect the SPH's guidance. Item III — that GIPS compliance alone is insufficient — is directly supported by the Jackson example.

Option C is incorrect because Item II is directly contradicted by the Jackson example, which establishes that relying solely on GIPS compliance and performance data violates Standard V(A) regardless of time pressure.

topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — Disclosures for Non-GIPS Firms
stem:
Consider the following disclosures that a member's firm — which does NOT claim GIPS compliance — may include to satisfy Standard III(D):

I. Tailoring the performance presentation based on the knowledge and sophistication of the audience
II. Disclosing that results are simulated when model results are used in the performance presentation
III. Disclosing whether the performance record presented is that of a prior entity rather than the current firm

How many of the above are explicitly identified in the SPH as methods of meeting Standard III(D) obligations for non-GIPS-compliant firms?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the SPH enumerates tailoring presentations to audience sophistication and providing specific disclosures — including simulation status and prior-entity attribution — as valid III(D) methods

C is correct. The SPH states that non-GIPS firms may satisfy Standard III(D) by "considering the knowledge and sophistication of the audience to whom a performance presentation is addressed when determining what information to provide and tailoring it accordingly" (Item I); by "including disclosures that fully explain the performance results being reported (for example, stating, when appropriate, that results are simulated when model results are used" (Item II); and by "clearly indicating when the performance record is that of a prior entity" (Item III). All three items correspond directly to explicit SPH provisions. All three satisfy the criterion.

Option A is incorrect. All three items are explicitly enumerated; identifying only one as satisfying the standard ignores the equally explicit audience-tailoring and simulated-results disclosures.

Option B is incorrect. All three practices appear in the same enumerated list in the SPH, making it inaccurate to exclude any one of them as a valid non-GIPS method of satisfying Standard III(D).

topic: Standard VII(A) Conduct as Participants in CFA Institute Programs — GIPS Technical Committee
stem:
Consider the following actions by Warrenski, a member of the CFA Institute GIPS Standards Technical Committee:

I. Using advance knowledge of confidential upcoming GIPS standard changes to promote her firm's services and imply a strategic advantage to clients
II. Factually stating to clients that she serves on the GIPS Standards Technical Committee when explaining her familiarity with GIPS requirements
III. Assisting clients in facilitating compliance with GIPS changes after those changes have been publicly released by CFA Institute

How many of the above actions constitute a violation of Standard VII(A) according to the SPH?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: Standard VII(A) is violated by using committee association to promote firm services; factual committee disclosure and post-release client assistance are permitted

A is correct. The SPH states that Warrenski "used her association with the GIPS Standards Technical Committee to promote her firm's services to clients and potential clients" and that "implying to clients that she would use confidential information to further their interests, Warrenski compromised the reputation and integrity of CFA Institute and thus violated Standard VII(A)." Item I therefore constitutes a violation. The SPH expressly permits the alternative: "She may factually state her involvement with the committee but cannot infer any special advantage to her clients from such participation." Item II (factual disclosure) is explicitly permitted. Item III — assisting with publicly released GIPS changes — involves no confidential information and no promotion of firm advantage; it does not fall within the SPH's description of the violation. Only Item I constitutes a violation.

Option B is incorrect because Item II is explicitly permitted by the SPH, which distinguishes factual disclosure of committee membership from the prohibited use of confidential committee knowledge to suggest a business advantage.

Option C is incorrect because Items II and III are not violations. Item II is explicitly sanctioned; Item III involves only publicly available information and does not implicate the promotion-of-firm-services concern at the heart of Standard VII(A).

topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — Cumulative Requirements When Claiming GIPS Compliance
stem:
Consider the following requirements that the SPH establishes for firms claiming compliance with the GIPS standards:

I. Firms must meet all the requirements of the GIPS standards
II. Firms must make all mandatory disclosures required by the GIPS standards
III. Firms must meet any other GIPS requirements that apply to that firm's specific situation

How many of the above are explicitly identified in the SPH as obligations that arise when a firm claims GIPS compliance?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the SPH gives a three-part formulation of GIPS compliance obligations in the Judd example — all three elements are stated in a single sentence

C is correct. The SPH states verbatim in the comment on the Judd example: "When claiming compliance with the GIPS standards, firms must meet all the requirements, make mandatory disclosures, and meet any other requirements that apply to that firm's specific situation." Item I (meet all requirements), Item II (make mandatory disclosures), and Item III (meet situation-specific requirements) each map directly to a clause of this sentence. All three are explicitly stated obligations. All three satisfy the criterion.

Option A is incorrect. The SPH presents the three obligations as a unified package — selecting only one clause misrepresents the SPH's cumulative formulation of what claiming GIPS compliance entails.

Option B is incorrect. The SPH uses a three-part conjunction: "all the requirements," "mandatory disclosures," and "any other requirements that apply to that firm's specific situation." All three are co-equal; excluding any one of them is inconsistent with the SPH's text.

topic: Standard IV(A) Loyalty — GIPS Compliance Software and Employer Property
stem:
Consider the following statements concerning the Madeline scenario from the SPH, in which an unpaid intern assists a firm's effort to achieve GIPS compliance:

I. Because Madeline did not receive monetary compensation, she is not considered an employee for purposes of Standard IV(A) and may take GIPS compliance software she developed
II. Madeline violates Standard IV(A) by copying and taking proprietary GIPS compliance software she helped develop, because she used firm resources in creating it
III. Work product created by an intern using firm resources — such as GIPS compliance software — constitutes employer property regardless of whether the intern receives monetary compensation

How many of the above statements accurately reflect the SPH's analysis of this scenario?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: unpaid interns who use firm resources are considered employees for Standard IV(A); employer property created with firm resources cannot be taken without permission

B is correct. The SPH states: "Even though Madeline does not receive monetary compensation for her services at Murdoch, she used firm resources in creating the software and is considered an employee because she receives compensation and benefits in the form of work experience and knowledge." Item I is therefore inaccurate — the SPH explicitly extends employee status to unpaid interns who use firm resources. Item II is accurate — "By copying the software, Madeline violated Standard IV(A) because she misappropriated Murdoch's property without permission." Item III is accurate — the SPH's reasoning treats work product created using firm resources as employer property. Items II and III are accurate.

Option A is incorrect because two statements — not one — accurately reflect the SPH. Both the finding of a violation (Item II) and the characterization of the software as employer property (Item III) are directly supported by the SPH's analysis.

Option C is incorrect because Item I is explicitly contradicted by the SPH, which states that Madeline is considered an employee despite the absence of monetary compensation and therefore cannot take firm property without permission.

topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — Identifying GIPS-Related Violations
stem:
Consider the following three scenarios involving performance presentation:

I. A firm circulates a performance report claiming GIPS compliance when composite returns are calculated using simple-average (equal) weighting of portfolio returns rather than asset weighting
II. A member at a firm claiming GIPS compliance unknowingly distributes a performance presentation containing a typographical error that overstates composite assets tenfold before the mistake is discovered
III. A member presents an advertisement attributing performance achieved at a prior employer to the current firm without identifying that the results were earned while at the prior entity

How many of the above scenarios constitute a violation of the Standards according to the SPH?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: asset-weighting violations and undisclosed prior-employer performance violate Standards; unintentional pre-discovery typographical errors do not

B is correct. Scenario I matches the Judd example: "The GIPS standards require firms to asset weight portfolio returns to calculate composite returns. Judd's violation is not from any misuse of the data but from publishing a performance report with her firm's false claim of GIPS compliance" — a violation of Standard III(D). Scenario II matches the Yao example: "Because Yao did not knowingly make the misrepresentation, however, he did not violate Standard I(C)" — the pre-discovery distribution of an unintentional error is explicitly not a violation. Scenario III matches the McCoy example, in which the SPH holds that advertising performance earned at a prior employer "does not identify the equity performance as being earned while at G&P" constitutes a violation of Standard III(D). Scenarios I and III constitute violations; Scenario II does not.

Option A is incorrect because two scenarios constitute violations. Scenario III (misattributing prior-employer performance) is as explicitly condemned in the SPH as Scenario I (false GIPS asset-weighting claim).

Option C is incorrect because Scenario II is specifically excluded from constituting a violation by the SPH, which conditions a Standard I(C) violation on knowledge of the misrepresentation at the time of distribution.

topic: Standard I(C) Misrepresentation and Standard III(D) — Correct Characterizations of GIPS Scenarios
stem:
Consider the following characterizations of Standards-related conclusions from SPH examples involving GIPS:

I. A member who unknowingly distributes a GIPS-compliant firm's performance report containing a typographical error prior to discovering it has violated Standard I(C)
II. A firm that calculates composite returns using equal weighting — rather than asset weighting — while claiming GIPS compliance has violated Standard III(D)
III. A member who factually discloses her membership on the CFA Institute GIPS Standards Technical Committee to clients has violated Standard VII(A)

How many of the above characterizations are accurate according to the SPH?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the SPH exonerates inadvertent pre-discovery distribution under I(C), condemns equal-weighted composites under III(D), and expressly permits factual committee disclosure under VII(A)

A is correct. Item I is inaccurate: the Yao example states explicitly that "Because Yao did not knowingly make the misrepresentation, however, he did not violate Standard I(C)" — the inadvertent, pre-discovery distribution of an error does not constitute an I(C) violation. Item II is accurate: the Judd example confirms that equal-weighting composite returns while claiming GIPS compliance violates Standard III(D). Item III is inaccurate: the Warrenski example states that "She may factually state her involvement with the committee" without violating Standard VII(A). Only Item II is an accurate characterization.

Option B is incorrect because only one characterization — Item II — is accurate. Item I mischaracterizes the SPH, which explicitly exonerates inadvertent pre-discovery errors under Standard I(C).

Option C is incorrect because Items I and III are each directly contradicted by the SPH. A violation of I(C) requires knowing misrepresentation, and factual committee disclosure is expressly permitted under VII(A).

topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — Specific Disclosures Enumerated for Non-GIPS Firms
stem:
Consider the following disclosures that a member at a non-GIPS-compliant firm might include in a performance presentation:

I. A disclosure stating whether the performance shown is gross of fees, net of fees, or after tax
II. A disclosure identifying the names and years of service of every portfolio manager who contributed to the composite's historical returns
III. A disclosure stating that performance results are simulated when model results have been used

How many of the above disclosures are explicitly listed in the SPH as examples of inclusions that help a non-GIPS-compliant firm meet its Standard III(D) obligations?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the SPH enumerates specific disclosure types — fee basis and simulation status are explicit; individual manager attribution is not mentioned

B is correct. The SPH identifies as qualifying disclosures: "stating, when appropriate, that results are simulated when model results are used" (Item III — accurate) and "disclosing whether the performance is gross of fees, net of fees, or after tax" (Item I — accurate). The SPH does not enumerate disclosing the individual names or tenures of portfolio managers contributing to a composite (Item II) as part of the qualifying-disclosure list. Items I and III satisfy the criterion; Item II does not.

Option A is incorrect because two disclosures — fee basis (Item I) and simulated-results notation (Item III) — are explicitly listed as qualifying disclosures in the SPH. Selecting only one ignores an equally explicit provision.

Option C is incorrect because Item II — individual manager attribution — is not enumerated in the SPH's list of disclosures that satisfy Standard III(D) for non-GIPS-compliant firms. Inferring its inclusion would go beyond the SPH text.

topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — Full Set of Non-GIPS Satisfactory Practices
stem:
Consider the following practices that a member at a non-GIPS-compliant firm might adopt when presenting performance history:

I. Considering the knowledge and sophistication of the audience when determining what performance information to provide and tailoring it accordingly
II. Presenting the performance of a composite of similar portfolios rather than using a single representative account
III. Including disclosures that fully explain performance results, such as disclosing whether performance is gross of fees, net of fees, or after tax

How many of the above practices are explicitly enumerated in the SPH as methods by which non-GIPS-compliant firms can satisfy Standard III(D)?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the SPH lists all three items within the same enumerated set of practices permitting non-GIPS firms to satisfy Standard III(D)

C is correct. The SPH states verbatim that non-GIPS firms may meet Standard III(D) obligations by "considering the knowledge and sophistication of the audience to whom a performance presentation is addressed when determining what information to provide and tailoring it accordingly" (Item I); "presenting the performance of a composite of similar portfolios rather than using a single representative account" (Item II); and "including disclosures that fully explain the performance results being reported (for example, stating, when appropriate, that results are simulated when model results are used; clearly indicating when the performance record is that of a prior entity; or disclosing whether the performance is gross of fees, net of fees, or after tax)" (Item III). All three items are explicitly enumerated. All three satisfy the criterion.

Option A is incorrect because all three practices appear in the same enumerated list in the SPH. Selecting only one misrepresents the scope of the SPH's guidance, which presents all three as co-equal methods of satisfying the standard.

Option B is incorrect because Items I, II, and III are each individually named in the SPH's enumeration. Excluding any one of them cannot be reconciled with the SPH's text.

topic: Standard III(D) Performance Presentation — General GIPS Guidance for Members and Candidates
stem:
Consider the following general statements about GIPS standards as presented in the SPH:

I. Compliance with the GIPS standards is described as the best method for members and candidates to meet their Standard III(D) obligations when showing the performance history of assets they manage
II. Members and candidates whose firms do not comply with the GIPS standards have no obligation under Standard III(D) to present fair, accurate, or complete investment performance history
III. Members and candidates should encourage their firms to comply with the GIPS standards

How many of the above statements accurately reflect the SPH's guidance on GIPS standards?
explanation:
First Principles Thinking: GIPS is the best III(D) method and members should encourage adoption, but non-GIPS firms still bear full III(D) obligations through alternative practices

B is correct. The SPH states: "compliance with the GIPS standards is the best method to meet their obligations under Standard III(D)" — confirming Item I. The SPH also states: "Members and candidates should encourage their firms to comply with the GIPS standards" — confirming Item III. Item II is directly contradicted by the SPH, which devotes substantial guidance to describing how non-GIPS-compliant firms must "present fair, accurate, and complete investment performance history" through alternative practices such as composite presentations, inclusion of terminated accounts, and specific disclosures. Non-compliance with GIPS does not extinguish Standard III(D) obligations. Items I and III are accurate.

Option A is incorrect because two statements — not one — accurately reflect SPH guidance. The obligation on members to encourage GIPS adoption (Item III) is stated alongside the characterization of GIPS as the best III(D) method (Item I).

Option C is incorrect because Item II is directly refuted by the SPH. A substantial portion of the Standard III(D) guidance is devoted specifically to articulating how non-GIPS-compliant firms remain obligated — and how they may discharge that obligation through alternative practices.
